lesson.name: Academic Success and College Skills - Lesson 3: Academic Success (Bartlett et al.)

tasks.chapterTitle: Successful Foundations: English, Maths, Online Learning, and Information Literacy
Successful Futures: Employment and Life After Graduation

messages.content:
Based on the provided text, here is a concise summary of **Lesson 3: Academic Success** (Bartlett et al.):

### **Overview**
*Academic Success* acts as a comprehensive handbook for tertiary students, designed to support their transition into university and provide a toolkit for thriving throughout their studies. The text aims to empower students by building foundational skills that reduce academic stress and improve performance across all subjects.

### **Key Components**

*   **Successful Beginnings (Part A):** Focuses on the importance of the university experience, highlighting the value of a degree for long-term job stability and personal growth. It emphasizes adopting a "growth mindset"—the belief that abilities can be developed through effort—to improve resilience and grit.
*   **Successful Foundations (Part B):** Provides essential preparation for students, including English language foundations, navigating university resources, and understanding academic integrity.
*   **Successful Study Skills (Part C):** Offers practical, evidence-based methods for managing a student’s workload, including:
    *   Goal setting and time management.
    *   Combating procrastination.
    *   Optimizing study spaces.
    *   Effective reading, note-taking, and critical, analytical, and creative thinking.
*   **Successful Assessment (Part D):** Guides students through the practical requirements of assignments, presentations, and exam preparation to ensure their efforts translate into high-quality academic outcomes.
*   **Successful Futures (Part E):** Encourages students to look beyond graduation by focusing on career readiness, professional development, and navigating the job market.

### **Core Tenets of Success**
*   **Help-Seeking:** The text defines successful students as those who consistently utilize available resources, such as peer tutors, library services, and counseling [S.18].
*   **Adjustment:** Entering university involves multi-faceted adjustments—academic, cultural, emotional, financial, intellectual, and social [S.20]. Recognizing these as natural parts of the transition is key to success.
*   **Support Systems:** Students are encouraged to connect with peers, academic staff, and professional support teams to navigate the challenges of their studies together [S.18].

***

**Reference Sources:**
*   [S.13] Introduction to the purpose and structure of the text.
*   [S.15, S.16, S.17, S.18] Key concepts of the "growth mindset" and "help-seeking behaviors."
*   [S.20, S.21] Definitions of the six primary areas of adjustment during the first year of university.

Lesson 3: Academic Success (Bartlett et al.) - Presentation9 slides
Academic Success: A Student Guide
  • Understanding university expectations for academic achievement
  • Developing strategies for personal and professional growth

This document provides an overview of the open textbook "Academic Success," developed by the University of Southern Queensland to help tertiary students transition into and excel within their university studies [S.1, S.10].

Key points regarding the text include:

Purpose and Origin
The book is a derivative of "College Success" by Amy Baldwin, adapted specifically for an Australian educational context [S.10]. It is published via the LibreTexts OER platform, aiming to reduce financial barriers to education while providing a comprehensive guide for students navigating university life [S.3, S.10].

Structure and Content
The text is organized into five thematic sections designed to support students through their academic lifecycle:
Part A (Successful Beginnings): Covers adjusting to university life and building necessary support networks [S.4, S.13].
Part B (Successful Foundations): Focuses on essential academic skills, including English language proficiency, information literacy, and academic integrity [S.4, S.13].
Part C (Successful Study Skills): Provides practical strategies for time management, goal setting, reading, and critical thinking [S.4, S.13].
Part D (Successful Assessment): Offers guidance on excelling in various forms of assessment, such as assignments, presentations, and examinations [S.4, S.13].
Part E (Successful Futures): Addresses career preparation and life transitions after graduation [S.5, S.13].

Accessibility and Licensing
The resource reflects a strong commitment to accessibility, featuring compatibility with screen-readers, keyboard navigation, and alternative formats (PDF, EPUB) [S.11]. It is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License [S.12].

Contributors
The book is a collaborative effort by learning advisors and librarians from the University of Southern Queensland, with additional support from organizations like the National Science Foundation and the U.S. Department of Education [S.3, S.10]. The authors emphasize that mastery of these skills serves as a foundational investment for all subjects [S.13].

This summary covers the provided text on "Successful Beginnings" (pp. [S.14-19]).

### Introduction to University
Choosing to attend university is a significant commitment that serves as a pathway to achieving personal and professional goals. Beyond academic content, university provides a unique, low-stakes environment to develop essential career-readiness competencies, such as critical thinking, communication, teamwork, and digital literacy (p. [S.15-16]). University graduates generally benefit from increased job satisfaction, stability, and higher long-term earning potential (p. [S.15, 18]).

### The Growth Mindset
Success is heavily influenced by one's psychological approach to learning:
* Fixed Mindset: Viewing abilities as static and unchangeable.
* Growth Mindset: Believing that abilities can be developed through effort and learning (p. [S.16]).

Adopting a growth mindset fosters "grit" and persistence. Students who embrace this perspective are more likely to view challenges as opportunities, persist through initial failures, and ultimately increase their probability of academic success (p. [S.17]).

### The Importance of Seeking Help
A critical secret to university success is the proactive use of support systems. Successful students engage in "help-seeking behaviors" and self-advocacy by utilizing available university resources (p. [S.18]). Common areas for support include:
* Academic: Libraries and tutoring centers for subject-specific mastery.
* Health: Campus medical or wellness centers.
* Social: Student organizations and interest groups for community building.
* Financial: Support offices for guidance on funding and expenses (p. [S.18]).

### Conclusion
The journey to earning a degree is a collaborative effort involving students, academic staff, and professional support teams. By setting clear goals, maintaining a growth mindset, and actively seeking assistance, students can successfully navigate the transition to university life (p. [S.18]).

This summary covers the primary transitions and expectations for students entering university as outlined in sections [S.20] through [S.25].

**The Six Adjustment Areas**
Students typically navigate six key areas of transition:
Academic: Managing increased learning demands and seeking help early [S.20].
Cultural: Adapting to campus language, customs, and diverse environments [S.20].
Emotional: Developing resilience to navigate the highs and lows of university life [S.20].
Financial: Learning to manage new living costs and educational investments [S.20].
Intellectual: Engaging with new ideas and developing different ways of thinking [S.21].
Social: Building professional and personal relationships with lecturers and peers [S.21].

**University Culture and Self-Responsibility**
University acts as a distinct culture with specific terminology (e.g., course specifications, attendance policies, and plagiarism guidelines). Crucially, learning is now the student’s responsibility. Success requires self-motivation, effective time management, and the ability to balance autonomous study with classroom requirements [S.22].

**Practical Experiences and Challenges**
Certain programs require field placements—such as student teaching or clinical rotations—which serve as real-world classrooms [S.23-24]. Regardless of the major, students often face hurdles, including imposter syndrome, anxiety regarding mistakes, and the urge to handle all pressures alone. It is highly recommended that students prioritize mental and physical health and utilize available university resources to overcome these challenges [S.25].

**Financial Support**
Students are encouraged to seek "free money" through grants and scholarships, which do not require repayment. These may be provided by private foundations, organizations, or employers through tuition reimbursement programs [S.24].

Navigating university can be challenging, but success is achievable by proactively building a support network and utilizing available institutional resources [S.26]. 

Key strategies for success include:

Building a Support Network: 
Students should curate a list of contacts to assist with academic and personal planning [S.27]. Essential roles include academic advisors, who help navigate degree requirements and institutional policies, and mentors, who offer career guidance and model professional values [S.27, S.28, S.29].

Engaging with Faculty and Peers: 
Treating lecturers as learning partners—by communicating directly and expressing interest in your own education—improves the academic experience [S.28]. Similarly, peers and peer mentors provide invaluable academic and social support, often through collaborative programs or the practice of "yarning," which fosters knowledge sharing and community [S.29, S.30].

Inclusivity and Support for Students with Disabilities: 
Australian universities are governed by the Disability Discrimination Act 1992 (Cth), which mandates equal access to education [S.30]. Universities provide dedicated support teams and reasonable adjustments to ensure all students can demonstrate their capabilities [S.30].

Mental Health and Wellness: 
The transition to university can be overwhelming, leading to stress and anxiety [S.30]. It is vital to recognize these feelings and access university counseling or health services, as they can provide necessary support or off-campus referrals for those struggling with mental health [S.30, S.31]. 

Ultimately, seeking help is a sign of strength rather than weakness, and establishing these connections is essential for maintaining well-being and achieving academic goals [S.26, S.31].

This section outlines the essential foundations for academic success at the university level. Below is a structured summary of the key points:

**Academic English and Writing Basics**
Proficiency in academic English is fundamental to success. Students should prioritize clarity and conciseness, generally aiming for sentences between 15–25 words (p. 35). Key rules include writing in the third person, avoiding slang, colloquialisms, and overly emotive language, and refraining from using contractions (pp. 35-36). Referencing sources is mandatory to credit research, and planning is considered a critical initial step for any assignment (p. 35).

**Grammar and Structure**
Understanding parts of speech—such as nouns, verbs, adjectives, and conjunctions—is vital for constructing effective sentences (p. 36). To maintain engagement, students should use a variety of sentence structures:
- Simple: One subject and one predicate (p. 36).
- Compound: At least two independent clauses joined by conjunctions (p. 37).
- Complex: One independent clause and one dependent clause (p. 37).
- Compound-complex: A combination of the above (p. 37).

**Punctuation and Transitioning**
Correct punctuation, including the use of periods, commas, colons, semi-colons, and brackets, is essential for logical flow (p. 38). Transition words are used to connect ideas, provide contrast, show cause and effect, or conclude arguments (p. 39).

**Tenses and Subject-Verb Agreement**
Consistency in verb tense is required, especially when referencing research:
- Simple present: Used for stating main points or general opinions (p. 39).
- Simple past: Used for reporting past research findings (pp. 39-40).
- Present perfect: Used for ongoing research (p. 39).
Furthermore, subject-verb agreement must be maintained, ensuring that the verb matches the singular or plural form of the subject (p. 40).

Academic integrity is the cornerstone of university success, defined by honesty, trust, fairness, respect, and responsibility in all academic pursuits [S.71]. To maintain integrity, students must adhere to the following principles:

**Appropriate Referencing**
Consistent attribution of ideas, words, and data is essential. Whether using author-date styles (which include in-text citations and a reference list) or numbered styles (using footnotes or endnotes), students must identify original sources accurately [S.72]. Bibliographic software can assist, but manual practice is necessary to ensure accuracy [S.72].

**Avoiding Academic Misconduct**
Threats to integrity include plagiarism, self-plagiarism, collusion, contract cheating, and data fabrication [S.73, S.74]. 
- Plagiarism: Avoided through rigorous note-taking and correct attribution [S.73].
- Self-plagiarism: Each assignment must represent new intellectual effort [S.73].
- Collusion: Requires careful management when working with peers; share only for feedback, not for content generation [S.74].
- Contract cheating: Hiring others to complete work is unethical, undermines the value of a degree, and creates risks of blackmail [S.74].
- Data fabrication: Honesty in reporting findings is critical; unexpected results should be analyzed rather than altered [S.74].

**Support and Tools**
Universities provide resources such as writing centers, guides, and text-matching software (e.g., Turnitin) [S.74, S.75]. Using these tools to identify and correct potential errors before final submission is a professional and proactive way to uphold integrity [S.75].

By focusing on original contribution and transparent sourcing, students fulfill the expected academic outcomes and protect the integrity of their qualifications [S.75].

This section of the text focuses on developing successful study skills through intentional planning, self-regulation, and effective management techniques.

**Goals and Motivation**
Setting goals provides direction and serves as a powerful motivator. To be effective, goals should be structured using the SMART framework:
- Specific: Clearly defined objectives.
- Measurable: Quantifiable outcomes.
- Attainable: Realistic and within your capability.
- Relevant: Aligned with your academic needs.
- Time-bound: Equipped with a specific deadline. 

Successful students also cultivate "grit"—the persistence to continue toward a goal despite obstacles—which research suggests is a stronger predictor of academic achievement than innate talent (p. 79-80).

**Goal Hierarchy**
Goals function best when organized into long-term and short-term components. Long-term goals represent major, years-long aspirations, while short-term goals act as the essential action steps or "chunks" that build toward those larger objectives (p. 80-81).

**Prioritisation Strategies**
Effective time management relies on shifting away from reactive, emotion-driven choices toward logical prioritisation. A recommended tool is the Eisenhower Decision Matrix, which categorizes tasks based on two criteria:
1. Urgency: How quickly the task must be completed.
2. Importance: The overall value or long-term consequence of the task.

When facing conflicting priorities, students should evaluate the foundational value of each task and communicate with instructors if a deadline cannot be met (p. 81-83).

**Task Completion**
Efficiency is achieved by breaking complex tasks into smaller, dependent components. By analyzing the requirements of an activity—such as preparing for a lecture—before beginning, you can ensure that each step is completed in the correct, most logical order (p. 83).

Successfully managing university assessments involves a systematic approach that begins before you start researching or writing. By following these steps, you can reduce stress and improve your grades:

Understanding Requirements
Start by thoroughly analyzing your assessment task sheet. Identify topic words (what to research), task words (how to approach the assignment), and limiting words (scope and parameters). Review the marking rubric to understand exactly what criteria are being assessed and how marks are weighted. This helps you prioritize your time, focusing on tasks with higher point values (p. 131, 132).

Planning and Research
Create a plan or concept map to organize your ideas once you understand the task. Effective time management ensures you have dedicated blocks for research, writing, and, crucially, reviewing (p. 132). When searching for information, ensure sources are credible and match requirements regarding type and recency (p. 133). 

Writing and Reviewing
Focus on your outlined structure during the writing phase to stay on track. Before submission, set your work aside for a few days if possible; this allows you to review with "fresh eyes." Use the rubric as a checklist to verify that your work meets formatting, word count, and content expectations (p. 133).

The Role of Feedback
The assessment process continues after submission. View feedback as a constructive tool for development rather than a personal critique. Engaging with feedback involves staying open-minded, reflecting on comments, and applying those insights to future assignments to avoid repeating mistakes. If an assignment is worth $$25\%$$ of your total grade and you receive $$80/100$$, your contribution to the final grade is calculated as: 
$$ (80 / 100) \times 25\% = 20\% $$ 
Using feedback effectively is essential for ongoing improvement throughout your university studies (p. 134, 135).

This text outlines effective strategies for university students to transition successfully into professional employment. Key takeaways include:

**Professional Identity and Networking**
Start building a professional identity from the beginning of your studies. Act as a professional by engaging positively in classes, tutorials, and forums. Foster relationships with lecturers, as they can provide industry insights and academic references. Additionally, join professional organizations, participate in mentoring programs, and attend industry events to expand your network (p. 187, 188, 190).

**Skills Development and Experience**
Beyond academic knowledge, employers value soft skills such as communication, teamwork, problem-solving, and adaptability. Enhance your employability by seeking internships, work-integrated learning, or volunteer roles. Even unrelated part-time work helps demonstrate reliability and professionalism. Engaging in extra-curricular activities, such as student clubs or leadership positions, further rounds out your profile (p. 188, 189, 191).

**Digital and Professional Presence**
Maintain a professional digital footprint. Be mindful that employers may screen social media profiles; consider keeping personal and professional accounts separate. Create a high-quality resume, and where applicable, develop a professional portfolio to showcase your work and achievements (p. 187, 189).

**Preparation and Support**
Actively research the requirements of your chosen profession, including necessary registrations or accreditation. Utilize university resources, such as career service departments, which offer support with resume writing, interview preparation, and job searches. Developing a checklist of required skills and preparing for applications well in advance—often months before graduation—will significantly increase your competitiveness in the job market (p. 186, 188, 189, 191).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the introduction, what is the primary purpose of the 'Academic Success' book?
options: To provide specific course content for mechanical engineering and clinical psychology students
To teach essential skills for university students to thrive and manage the demands of tertiary study
To act as a comprehensive guide for navigating university administrative enrollment procedures
To replace the need for traditional textbooks and lectures in all university subjects
questions.explanation: The text states that the book is designed to help students master core skills—such as study techniques and assessment management—to empower them in all subjects throughout their university journey.

questions.question: What distinguishes a 'growth mindset' from a 'fixed mindset' in the context of academic learning?
options: A growth mindset prioritizes career-readiness competencies over academic grades
A growth mindset assumes that intelligence is innate and cannot be altered through effort
A growth mindset views abilities as skills that can be developed and learned over time
A growth mindset is only applicable to students returning to study after time in the workforce
questions.explanation: According to the text, a growth mindset involves the belief that abilities are not static; rather, they can be learned, developed, and improved through effort.

questions.question: Why does the text argue that university is a unique environment for developing 'soft skills' like teamwork, leadership, and problem-solving?
options: Because students are required to master these skills before they are allowed to graduate
Because these skills are taught in dedicated workshops separate from academic coursework
Because university provides a low-stakes environment where one can learn without the fear of being fired
Because students at university are exclusively evaluated on soft skills rather than academic performance
questions.explanation: The text highlights that university allows students to develop essential career-readiness competencies in a 'low-stakes' environment, unlike a workplace setting where errors may have professional consequences.

questions.question: If a student finds themselves questioning the relevance of a specific course to their major, what approach does the text recommend?
options: Contacting the university administration to request a curriculum change
Focusing only on courses that directly align with their future job title
Looking for connections between the course content and their broader long-term goals
Asking the lecturer to clarify why the course is a mandatory requirement
questions.explanation: The text advises students to challenge themselves to find connections between the course content and their larger educational, career, and life goals rather than assuming it is irrelevant.

questions.question: According to the introduction, what is the significance of the 'student stories' included in the book?
options: They provide official testimonials used for the university's marketing materials
They offer personal reflections from past and current students about their university experiences
They serve as placeholders to make the chapters more visually appealing
They provide detailed case studies that students are expected to analyze for their exams
questions.explanation: The text describes student stories as short quotes from diverse students reflecting on their personal experiences, which helps add context to the academic advice provided.

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of 'Academic Success' by Bartlett et al.?
cards.back: To provide students with a handbook of core skills to help them transition into, survive, and thrive in their university studies.

cards.front: What is the 'fixed mindset' according to Dr. Carol Dweck?
cards.back: The belief that abilities are inherent traits that one either possesses or does not, and cannot be changed or improved.

cards.front: What is the 'growth mindset' according to Dr. Carol Dweck?
cards.back: The belief that abilities are qualities that can be learned, developed, and improved through effort.

cards.front: What are four potential benefits of attending university mentioned in the text?
cards.back: Greater job satisfaction, better job stability, better outcomes for the next generation, and greater earning potential over time.

cards.front: Name four of the eight career-readiness competencies identified by the National Association of Colleges and Employers.
cards.back: Critical thinking/problem solving, oral/written communication, teamwork/collaboration, and digital technology (others: leadership, professionalism/work ethic, career management, global/intercultural fluency).

cards.front: What are the five parts that comprise the 'Academic Success' text?
cards.back: 1. Successful Beginnings, 2. Successful Foundations, 3. Successful Study Skills, 4. Successful Assessment, and 5. Successful Futures.

cards.front: How does 'Academic Success' describe the role of student stories in the book?
cards.back: They are short quotes from past and current students from diverse backgrounds, reflecting on their personal experiences at university.

cards.front: Why is the university environment considered 'low-stakes' for developing soft skills?
cards.back: It provides opportunities to practice and develop professional skills (like leadership or teamwork) without the fear of being fired.

cards.front: What is the origin of the 'Academic Success' text?
cards.back: It is a derivative of 'College Success' by Amy Baldwin, adapted and re-worked for an Australian university context by the team at the University of Southern Queensland.

cards.front: How does the 'Academic Success' text encourage students to view courses that seem unrelated to their major?
cards.back: Students are encouraged to challenge themselves to look for connections between the course content and their larger educational, career, and life goals.

slides.title: Academic Success: A Student Guide
bullets: Understanding university expectations for academic achievement
Developing strategies for personal and professional growth
slides.notes: This session introduces the framework for achieving success throughout your university journey. We will cover mindset, support, and essential foundational skills.

slides.title: Part 1: Successful Beginnings
bullets: Reflecting on personal motivations and goals
Developing a 'growth mindset' for persistent learning
Connecting with staff and peers for support
Value of grit in overcoming early challenges (p. 17)
slides.notes: Success starts with your mindset; viewing abilities as learnable traits helps you navigate the challenges of transitioning to university life.

slides.title: Part 2: Adjusting to University
bullets: Navigating academic, social, and emotional adjustments
Understanding university language and institutional culture
Developing strategies for healthy life-work-study balance
Recognizing the transformative nature of higher education
slides.notes: University is a cultural experience requiring adjustments. Embracing these changes is key to intellectual and personal transformation.

slides.title: Part 3: Successful Connections
bullets: Seeking help is a proactive sign of strength
Building a formal support and accountability network
Utilizing academic advisors for degree planning (p. 28)
Engaging lecturers as vital learning partners
slides.notes: You don't have to navigate university alone; creating a robust support network is a critical strategy for your long-term success.

slides.title: Part 4: Successful Foundations
bullets: Mastering academic English basics for assessment
Writing clearly, concisely, and in the third person
Adhering to formal academic language standards
Planning writing tasks before beginning research (p. 35)
slides.notes: Academic literacy is a foundational language you learn during your studies. Focus on clarity, formal tone, and careful planning.

slides.title: Part 5: Integrity at University
bullets: Academic integrity requires honesty, trust, and fairness
Consistent attribution of sources via referencing
Understanding author-date (APA) vs. numbered styles (p. 72)
Using bibliographic software to assist management
slides.notes: Integrity underpins everything you do. Accurate referencing isn't just a requirement; it is a demonstration of academic ethics.

slides.title: Part 6: Successful Study Skills
bullets: Setting SMART goals to drive motivation (p. 78)
Applying grit to remain focused on long-term outcomes
Breaking complex tasks into manageable components
Balancing deadlines with necessary flexibility
slides.notes: Productivity is intentional. By setting SMART goals and managing your priorities, you transform abstract desires into concrete results.

slides.title: Part 7: Successful Assessment
bullets: Analyzing task sheets to identify requirements
Dissecting topic, task, and limiting words (p. 130)
Planning structures before drafting assignments
Using feedback to improve future submissions
slides.notes: Effective assessment management starts with interpreting the task correctly. Don't rush; analyze your criteria to ensure your effort is focused.

slides.title: Part 8: Successful Futures
bullets: Adopting a 'professional in training' identity now
Building relationships with lecturers for networking
Managing your professional social media presence
Engaging with career practitioners for guidance (p. 186)
slides.notes: Preparation for your career begins on day one. By curating your professional image and identity, you set yourself up for post-graduation success.
